rationales.stimulation: He pushes back on the idea that he should make a bigger holiday plan and prefers staying home over doing something more novel.
rationales.security: He says the house and routines he built with Linda took a long time to feel settled and are worth protecting, showing clear attachment to stability.

rationales.stimulation: He talks himself out of the greenfield migration and frames taking it as 'chasing something shiny,' rejecting the novelty of the new project.
rationales.security: What stops him is concern about the two maintenance systems only he understands, prioritizing continuity and operational stability.

rationales.achievement: Even while 'running on fumes,' he spends the day proactively closing out tickets nobody assigned him so the work ends the year fully complete.
rationales.security: He cannot stand the thought of entering January with stale statuses and 'loose ends,' showing a strong pull toward order and predictability.

rationales.achievement: He calmly diagnoses the Jenkins failure, fixes it in twenty minutes, and feels good because his years of competence 'meant something concrete.'
rationales.security: The satisfaction comes from knowing this familiar failure pattern and feeling 'exactly where I should be' after twenty-two years in the same shop.
He rereads the benefit options, keeps the same predictable PPO, maxes the HSA, stays bond-heavy, and says he'd rather leave money on the table than lose sleep.
rationales.benevolence: He bases the insurance and dental planning on Linda's prescription needs and upcoming crown, organizing the benefits around his wife's welfare.

rationales.achievement: He is relieved that Marcus got the ambitious microservices project and admits he may have stopped reaching for opportunities like that too early.
rationales.security: His relief centers on his systems still running plus the pension vesting, house, Linda's coverage, and Roth plan that give him a stable path to sixty-two.
